executive_brief:
The cybersecurity landscape has fundamentally shifted this week. The traditional 'patch window'—the time between vulnerability disclosure and exploitation—has collapsed to near zero. AI-driven vulnerability discovery, exemplified by Anthropic's Claude Mythos, is identifying thousands of zero-days, while criminal groups like Storm-1175 are weaponizing them within 24 hours of disclosure [138], [77]. Simultaneously, the software supply chain is under sustained assault. The compromise of the Axios NPM maintainer account and the hijacking of CPUID's download servers demonstrate that trust in package registries and vendor infrastructure is broken [1], [296]. 

State-sponsored actors are escalating beyond digital espionage into physical disruption. Iran-linked groups are actively targeting Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in US critical infrastructure, causing real-world outages [142], [154]. Meanwhile, APT28 has weaponized home routers to conduct massive DNS hijacking campaigns [100]. The convergence of AI-accelerated attacks, fragile supply chains, and physical infrastructure targeting creates a threat environment where speed and resilience are the only viable defenses.

threat_stories.headline: The Zero-Day Timeline Collapses: AI Accelerates Exploitation
threat_stories.narrative:
The era of the 'patch Tuesday' safety net is over. This week, the gap between vulnerability discovery and active exploitation has vanished. Anthropic's Claude Mythos AI model has autonomously identified thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across major operating systems and browsers, fundamentally changing the vulnerability discovery landscape [132], [145]. While this offers defensive potential, it signals that offensive capabilities are equally accessible. 

Criminal groups are already leveraging this acceleration. Storm-1175, linked to the Medusa ransomware group, is exploiting zero-days and N-day vulnerabilities to deploy ransomware within 24 hours of a flaw's disclosure [22], [77]. This velocity renders traditional vulnerability management cycles obsolete. Attackers are chaining vulnerabilities, such as combining zero-days with misconfigurations, to bypass security controls before defenders can react [87]. 

The immediate impact is seen in the Marimo RCE flaw (CVE-2026-39987), which was exploited in the wild within 10 hours of public disclosure [278], [313]. This pattern indicates that automated scanning and exploitation bots are now the primary threat vector, not just human-led campaigns. Organizations relying on manual patching schedules are effectively defenseless against this new tempo.

timeline.event: Anthropic unveils Claude Mythos, revealing thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities discovered by AI.
timeline.significance: Marks the transition to AI-driven vulnerability discovery at scale.

timeline.event: Storm-1175 exploits zero-days to deploy Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of disclosure.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates criminal adaptation to AI-accelerated threat landscape.

timeline.event: Marimo RCE (CVE-2026-39987) exploited within 10 hours of disclosure.
timeline.significance: Confirms automated exploitation is now faster than human patching.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. All internet-facing systems and development environments are at risk. The mean time-to-exploit has dropped from 61 days to 28.5 days, with zero-days weaponized almost instantly [206].
threat_stories.action_required: Implement automated patching for critical CVEs. Deploy AI-driven vulnerability management tools. Isolate critical systems from the internet where possible. Assume zero-day exploitation is ongoing and hunt for anomalies rather than waiting for patches.

threat_stories.headline: Supply Chain Trust Broken: Axios, CPUID, and the Poisoned Well
threat_stories.narrative:
The software supply chain is no longer a trusted conduit for updates; it is a primary attack vector. This week, the Axios NPM package maintainer account was hijacked, allowing threat actors to push malicious versions to the repository [1], [10]. This is not an isolated incident. The CPUID website was compromised to serve malware via HWMonitor and CPU-Z downloads, affecting millions of users [296], [302]. Additionally, 36 malicious npm packages disguised as Strapi CMS plugins were discovered deploying persistent implants [14].

These attacks share a common trait: they compromise the integrity of the delivery mechanism itself. The Axios hack involved a fake Teams error fix to hijack the maintainer account [10], while the CPUID attack poisoned the vendor's own update servers [257]. The Smart Slider 3 Pro plugin was also hijacked, distributing backdoored updates to over 800,000 installations [252].

The implication is clear: developers and system administrators can no longer trust the source of their software. Dependency injection and supply chain attacks are now sophisticated, targeted, and capable of bypassing standard integrity checks.

timeline.event: Axios NPM maintainer account hijacked via fake Teams error fix.
timeline.significance: Compromises a foundational web development library.

timeline.event: 36 malicious npm packages disguised as Strapi plugins discovered.
timeline.significance: Shows scale of automated supply chain poisoning.

timeline.event: CPUID site hijacked to serve malware via HWMonitor downloads.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Any organization using npm, WordPress, or vendor-provided hardware tools is at risk. The blast radius includes downstream applications relying on compromised libraries.
threat_stories.action_required: Pin dependency versions in all projects. Use private npm registries with vetted packages. Audit all third-party dependencies for integrity. Verify software downloads via checksums from multiple sources.

threat_stories.headline: Physical Disruption: Iran-Linked Actors Target Critical Infrastructure PLCs
threat_stories.narrative:
State-sponsored cyber warfare has moved from data theft to physical disruption. Iran-linked threat actors, including groups like CyberAv3ngers, are actively targeting internet-exposed Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in US critical infrastructure [142], [154]. These attacks target Rockwell Automation and Siemens devices, exploiting insecure configurations to gain remote access. 

Unlike typical espionage, these actors are manipulating SCADA and HMI displays, altering project files, and installing remote access software to maintain persistence [154]. At least 75 devices have been compromised in related campaigns since November 2023 [142]. The goal is operational disruption, potentially causing outages in energy, water, and manufacturing sectors. 

The vulnerability lies in the exposure of legacy protocols like Modbus and DNP3 to the public internet without proper segmentation [204]. Attackers use leased overseas infrastructure to connect to these devices, bypassing traditional perimeter defenses. This represents a direct threat to national security and public safety.

timeline.event: FBI/CISA issue joint advisory on Iran-linked actors targeting PLCs.
timeline.significance: Official warning of active targeting of OT environments.

timeline.event: Real-world disruption confirmed at US critical infrastructure sites.
timeline.significance: Moves threat from theoretical to active physical impact.

timeline.event: Nearly 4,000 US industrial devices found exposed to Iranian cyberattacks.
timeline.significance: Reveals massive scale of vulnerable infrastructure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Energy, water, and manufacturing facilities with internet-exposed PLCs are at immediate risk of physical disruption or sabotage.
threat_stories.action_required: Disconnect PLCs from the internet or protect with firewalls. Block traffic on OT-associated ports (44818, 2222, 102, 22, 502) from overseas hosting providers. Implement network segmentation to isolate OT environments.

threat_stories.headline: The Router Espionage Network: APT28 Hijacks Home Infrastructure
threat_stories.narrative:
Russian state-sponsored group APT28 (Forest Blizzard) is executing a massive espionage campaign by compromising home and small-office routers. Since at least May 2025, the group has targeted MikroTik and TP-Link devices to hijack DNS settings [100], [166]. This turns compromised routers into malicious infrastructure for Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) attacks.

By redirecting DNS traffic, APT28 can intercept TLS connections, specifically targeting Microsoft Outlook on the web and other Microsoft 365 logins [158], [110]. The attackers use utilities like dnsmasq to spoof DNS responses and present invalid TLS certificates to steal credentials and emails [158]. This campaign undermines the security of remote work environments, as employees assume their home network is safe.

The US government has disrupted this operation, but the scale of the compromise suggests widespread infection [166], [174]. The use of SOHO routers as a staging ground for espionage highlights the vulnerability of the 'edge' of the network.

timeline.event: UK exposes Russian cyber unit hacking home routers to hijack internet traffic.
timeline.significance: Confirms state-sponsored use of consumer hardware for espionage.

timeline.event: US disrupts Russian espionage operation involving hacked routers and DNS hijacking.
timeline.significance: Government intervention confirms severity of threat.

timeline.event: Forest Blizzard leverages router compromises to launch AiTM attacks on Outlook sessions.
timeline.significance: Specific targeting of enterprise credentials via home infrastructure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Remote workers and SOHO environments are at risk. Compromised routers can bypass MFA and intercept sensitive communications.
threat_stories.action_required: Update router firmware immediately. Disable remote management (WAN access) to admin interfaces. Enforce DNS over HTTPS (DoH) on client devices. Use hardware tokens for authentication to mitigate credential theft.

actor_profiles.name: Storm-1175
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Exploiting zero-days and N-day vulnerabilities to deploy Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of disclosure. Chaining vulnerabilities for rapid post-compromise activity.

actor_profiles.name: DPRK-Nexus (Lazarus)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Supply chain attacks (Axios, npm), social engineering ($285M Drift hack), and phishing campaigns using LNK files and GitHub C2.

actor_profiles.name: APT28 (Forest Blizzard)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Hijacking SOHO routers for DNS hijacking and AiTM attacks. Targeting Microsoft 365 credentials and Ukraine/NATO infrastructure.

actor_profiles.name: Iran-Nexus (CyberAv3ngers)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Targeting internet-exposed PLCs in US critical infrastructure to cause operational disruption and data exfiltration.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control vulnerability allowing unauthenticated attackers to bypass authentication via API and escalate privileges. Actively exploited in the wild.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply emergency hotfix for EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6. Upgrade to version 7.4.7.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Pre-authenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) via WebSocket endpoint (/terminal/ws). Exploited within 10 hours of disclosure.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade Marimo to version 0.23.0 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection vulnerability in CustomMCP node allowing Remote Code Execution. 12,000+ instances exposed.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Flowise to the latest patched version immediately. Disable CustomMCP node.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authorization bypass allowing attackers to gain host access via oversized HTTP requests. Regression of CVE-2024-41110.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Docker Engine to version 29.3.1 or later. Docker Desktop to 4.66.1.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical code injection vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution. Added to CISA KEV catalog.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply the latest patch released by Ivanti immediately.

hunting_leads.title: DPRK LNK File Execution via GitHub C2
hunting_leads.context: DPRK actors are using LNK files to execute PowerShell scripts that connect to GitHub repositories for C2. Look for LNK execution followed by GitHub API traffic.
hunting_leads.query: process_name="lnk" OR process_name="powershell.exe" | where destination_ip in (github_api_ips) | where user_agent contains "Python"

hunting_leads.title: FortiClient EMS API Bypass
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-35616 allows unauthenticated API access. Monitor for API requests without valid tokens or from unusual IPs.
hunting_leads.query: event_type="api_request" AND product="FortiClient EMS" AND authentication_status="unauthenticated"

hunting_leads.title: PLC Traffic from Overseas Hosting
hunting_leads.context: Iran-linked actors target PLCs from overseas IPs. Monitor OT ports for traffic from non-local networks.
hunting_leads.query: destination_port in (502, 2222, 44818) AND source_country NOT IN (local_countries)
indicators: Connections to PLCs from cloud IPs

hunting_leads.title: NPM Supply Chain Anomalies
hunting_leads.context: Malicious npm packages are being injected. Monitor for unexpected package versions or postinstall scripts.
hunting_leads.query: package_manager="npm" AND action="install" AND package_name in (axios, strapi) AND version != expected_version
indicators: Connections to unknown C2 from npm processes

hunting_leads.title: Router DNS Hijacking
hunting_leads.context: APT28 hijacks DNS on SOHO routers. Look for DNS resolution changes or traffic to suspicious DNS servers.
hunting_leads.query: dns_query AND dns_server NOT IN (trusted_dns_servers) AND client_ip in (home_network_range)

{'summary': 'Malicious .cmd script campaign leveraging PowerShell for privilege escalation, AV bypass, and persistence via scheduled tasks.', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['Microsoft Windows', 'Windows Defender', 'PowerShell'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Disable script execution via Group Policy', 'Monitor for unauthorized Add-MpPreference commands', 'Block access to is.gd and search.app domains'], 'references': ['Securityaffairs: Image or Malware? Read until the end and answer in comments :)'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': "The article details a malware campaign delivered via phishing email containing a malicious .cmd file. The script performs privilege escalation using PowerShell 'RunAs', adds exclusions to Windows Defender to bypass antivirus, downloads a disguised payload (initially .jpg, actually .zip) from a shortened URL, extracts an executable, creates a hidden scheduled task for persistence, and self-deletes after rebooting the system. The specific underlying vulnerability exploited by the final payload is not disclosed, but the delivery mechanism relies on social engineering and script execution.", 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'functional', 'availability_impact': 'high', 'remediation': "Remove Windows Defender exclusions added by the malware, delete the malicious scheduled task ('\\Microsoft\\Windows\\IntelGraphicsTask'), remove the payload files from %LOCALAPPDATA%, and block the identified malicious URLs. Update antivirus definitions and ensure PowerShell execution policies are restricted.", 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': '', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'required', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-04-05', 'discovery_date': ''}

statistics.key_changes: Time-to-exploit for critical vulnerabilities has dropped from 61 days to 28.5 days. AI-driven vulnerability discovery is now a confirmed threat vector.
emerging_trends: Supply chain attacks targeting vendor infrastructure
declining_threats: Traditional spear-phishing (replaced by automated credential harvesting)
Manual vulnerability exploitation (replaced by automated scanning)
